perturbation.original_text:
22 LIABILITY
22.1 No Party excludes or restricts its liability for death or personal injury resulting from its own negligence. Subject to the preceding sentence, no Party shall be liable to any other Party in contract, tort or otherwise including any liability for negligence for any indirect or consequential loss or damage including, without limitation, corruption or loss of data, loss of profit, loss of anticipated savings all in connection with this Agreement, caused by its own acts or those of any of its auxiliaries, such as employees, servants or agents. Furthermore, no Party shall be liable to any other Party in contract, tort or otherwise for any direct damage unless and to the extent it is based on intent or gross negligence. In no event shall any employee, servant or agent of a Party be liable to another Party for any negligence or intent in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text:
22 LIABILITY
22.1 Subject to the preceding sentence, no Party shall be liable to any other Party in contract, tort or otherwise including any liability for negligence for any indirect or consequential loss or damage including, without limitation, corruption or loss of data, loss of profit, loss of anticipated savings all in connection with this Agreement, caused by its own acts or those of any of its auxiliaries, such as employees, servants or agents. Furthermore, no Party shall be liable to any other Party in contract, tort or otherwise for any direct damage unless and to the extent it is based on intent or gross negligence. In no event shall any employee, servant or agent of a Party be liable to another Party for any negligence or intent in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the clause 'No Party excludes or restricts its liability for death or personal injury resulting from its own negligence' attempts to waive liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence, which is generally prohibited by law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Limitation of Liability for Personal Injury or Death
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1668 states that contracts that exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury, or violation of the law are against public policy. Removing the clause that ensures liability for death or personal injury due to negligence attempts to circumvent this law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause 'No Party excludes or restricts its liability for death or personal injury resulting from its own negligence' directly contradicts California Civil Code § 1668, as cited in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that contracts designed to exempt someone from responsibility for their own negligence are against public policy. The changed text attempts to waive liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence, which is generally prohibited. This constitutes a legal contradiction, as it undermines legally mandated protections.

perturbation.original_text: 29.1 All data and information that is acquired or received by any Party in anticipation of or under this Agreement shall be confidential and shall not be divulged in any - -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- September 2, 1998 Page 30 way to any third party, without the prior written approval of the other Parties, nor shall it be used for any purpose beyond the scope of this Agreement. Any Party may, without such approval, disclose such data and information to: (i) the employees of that Party; or (ii) the extent required by any applicable laws, or the requirement of any recognized stock exchange in compliance with its rules and regulations or in the case if a party wholly owned by a sovereign government, by the rules of governance of the Party, or (iii) any government agency or regulatory authority lawfully requesting such information or to which such information needs to be submitted in order to obtain any necessary consent or approval', or (iv) any Court of competent jurisdiction acting in pursuance of its powers; or (v) professional advisors, auditors and bankers or any bona fide intending assignee upon obtaining a similar undertaking of confidentiality; or (vi) the extent that such data and/or information is generally available to the public. Any Party may disclose such data and information to such persons as may be necessary in connection with the conduct of operations of TAT-14 upon obtaining a similar undertaking of confidentiality from such persons.
perturbation.changed_text: 29.1 All data and information that is acquired or received by any Party in anticipation of or under this Agreement shall be confidential and shall not be divulged in any - -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- September 2, 1998 Page 30 way to any third party, without the prior written approval of the other Parties, nor shall it be used for any purpose beyond the scope of this Agreement. Any Party may, without such approval, disclose such data and information to: (i) the employees of that Party; or (ii) the extent required by any applicable laws, or the requirement of any recognized stock exchange in compliance with its rules and regulations or in the case if a party wholly owned by a sovereign government, by the rules of governance of the Party, or (iii) any government agency or regulatory authority lawfully requesting such information or to which such information needs to be submitted in order to obtain any necessary consent or approval', or (iv) any Court of competent jurisdiction acting in pursuance of its powers; or (v) professional advisors, auditors and bankers or any bona fide intending assignee upon obtaining a similar undertaking of confidentiality; or (vi) the extent that such data and/or information is generally available to the public.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'Any Party may disclose such data and information to such persons as may be necessary in connection with the conduct of operations of TAT-14 upon obtaining a similar undertaking of confidentiality from such persons.' restricts disclosure of data necessary for operations, potentially conflicting with obligations to report security breaches or cooperate with investigations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1798.29 requires businesses to notify California residents if their unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person. By restricting the disclosure of data necessary for operations, the contract hinders adherence to laws regarding data security breach notifications.

OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.8. PERSONAL DATA [1798 - 1798.78] ( Title 1.8 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) CHAPTER 1. Information Practices Act of 1977 [1798 - 1798.78] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) ARTICLE 7. Accounting of Disclosures [1798.25 - 1798.29] ( Article 7 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) 1798.29. (a) Any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of California (1) whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person, or, (2) whose encrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the encryption key or security credential was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the agency that owns or licenses the encrypted information has a reasonable belief that the encryption key or security credential could
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the clause that allows a party to disclose data and information necessary for the operations of TAT-14, provided they obtain a similar undertaking of confidentiality. The Law Explanation states that California Civil Code § 1798.29 requires businesses to notify California residents if their unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person. The omission restricts data disclosure necessary for operations, hindering adherence to data security breach notification laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS, states in section 1798.29 (a) that 'Any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of California'. Therefore, removing the ability to disclose data for operational needs, particularly in the context of potential security breaches, directly contradicts the legal obligation to report such breaches, creating a significant legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation also reinforces this by pointing out the potential conflict with obligations to report security breaches.

perturbation.original_text:
36 INTERPRETATION OF THE AGREEMENT AND SETTLEMENT OF DISPUTES
36.1 The construction, interpretation and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Switzerland, except for its conflicts of law principles.
36.2 Any dispute relating to this Agreement or its subject matter, including disputes as to validity, performance, breach, or termination, which cannot be settled by mutual agreement between the Parties, shall be submitted to binding arbitration under the Rules of Conciliation and Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce as in force on the date of the commencement of the arbitration and as modified by this arbitration clause. The appointing and administering body shall be the International Chamber of Commerce. There shall be only one arbitrator. The arbitration shall take place in Geneva, Switzerland, and the proceedings shall be conducted in the English language. The award shall be final and binding and the Parties hereby waive all means of recourse to the courts of - -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- September 2, 1998 Page 33 Switzerland or any other Country. Jucgment on the award may be entered in any court of competent Jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text:
36 INTERPRETATION OF THE AGREEMENT AND SETTLEMENT OF DISPUTES
36.1 The construction, interpretation and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Switzerland, except for its conflicts of law principles.
36.2 Any dispute relating to this Agreement or its subject matter, including disputes as to validity, performance, breach, or termination, which cannot be settled by mutual agreement between the Parties, shall be submitted to binding arbitration under the Rules of Conciliation and Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce as in force on the date of the commencement of the arbitration and as modified by this arbitration clause. The appointing and administering body shall be the International Chamber of Commerce. There shall be only one arbitrator. The arbitration shall take place in Geneva, Switzerland, and the proceedings shall be conducted in the English language. The award shall be final and binding and the Parties hereby waive all means of recourse to the courts of Switzerland or any other Country.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'Judgment on the award may be entered in any court of competent Jurisdiction' restricts the enforcement of the arbitration award within a court of law which can be problematic if enforcement is needed. This can conflict with the United States' commitment to enforcing international arbitration agreements and awards.
perturbation.law_explanation: 9 U.S. Code § 207 outlines the process for enforcing foreign arbitral awards in United States courts. By removing the clause that allows judgment on the award in a competent court, the contract makes it more difficult to enforce the award in the United States, potentially violating the spirit and intent of the Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards, as implemented by the U.S. Code.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 207 - Award of arbitrators; confirmation; jurisdiction; proceeding | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 207 - Award of arbitrators; confirmation; jurisdiction; proceeding U.S. Code Notes prev | next Within three years after an arbitral award falling under the Convention is made, any party to the arbitration may apply to any court having jurisdiction under this chapter for an order confirming the award as against any other party to the arbitration. The court shall confirm the award unless it finds one of the grounds for refusal or deferral of recognition or enforcement of the award specified in the said Convention. (Added Pub. L. 91–368, § 1 , July 31, 1970 , 84 Stat. 693 .) Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date Section effective upon the entry into force of the Convention on Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards with respect to the United States ( Dec. 29, 1970 ), see section 4 of Pub. L. 91–368 , set out as a note under section 201 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities How current is this?
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the clause allowing judgment on the arbitration award in a competent court. The perturbation explanation states that this "restricts the enforcement of the arbitration award within a court of law which can be problematic if enforcement is needed." 9 U.S. Code § 207 states that a party may apply to any court having jurisdiction for an order confirming the award. By removing the clause, the contract makes it more difficult to enforce the award in a U.S. court, therefore a legal contradiction exists.
